text: The surgeon positions the supraumbilical camera port 24 centimeters away from the target hernia site, following the baseball diamond concept. Two additional ipsilateral ports are placed 7.5 centimeters lateral to the camera port, with the right-hand port on the left side and the left-hand port on the right side, accommodating the left-sided inguinal hernia repair.

The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to gently reduce the contents of a large indirect inguinal scrotal hernia. The omentum is carefully pulled down into the abdomen with assistance, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The hernia contents are successfully removed from the sac and repositioned inside the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon begins dissection from the medial umbilical ligament using an ipsilateral port approach. The dissection progresses towards the arc of the hernia, reaching six centimeters to the outer margin of the deep ring at the ten o'clock position. A harmonic scalpel is used for precise cutting.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal pocket using a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia. The teflon jaw of the harmonic scalpel is positioned towards the abdominal wall to avoid damaging the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon begins by making a preperitoneal incision, carefully reducing the lateral and medial margins of the hernia defect. The area of the inferior epigastric vessel is visible, and the Teflon jaw of the harmonic scalpel is positioned towards the abdominal wall to prevent vessel injury. The incision starts at the medial umbilical ligament and extends six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the defect at the ten o'clock position.

The surgeon begins separating the peritoneum from the transversal fascia lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, focusing on the area known as the triangle of pain. Blunt dissection is used to carefully separate the areolar tissue, aided by insufflated gas.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, separating the peritoneum from the transversal fascia lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, preparing to proceed with the medial dissection.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection medial to the hernia sac, pushing the bladder inferiorly and medially to open the space of Retzius and visualize the Cooper's ligament. The inguinal scrotal hernia sac is carefully separated from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels, with the bladder continuously pushed medially and inferiorly.

The surgeon uses the left hand to apply traction inferomedially, revealing the Cooper ligament above the space of radius. Blunt dissection is performed to achieve reduction of the hernia sac, with the bladder being pushed inferomedially.
The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to carefully dissect the sac, pushing the transversal fascia away from it. The sac is being separated from the pseudo sac within the surgical field.

In the case of a large inguinal scrotal hernia, the surgeon may need to cut the sac due to dense adhesions, leaving part of it in the scrotum. The transverse fascia is visible above, while the sac is below. The sac is pulled inferomedially, and the transverse fascia is pushed upward. An assistant holds the testes to prevent them from being pulled up to the deep ring. The annular space is visible as the transverse fascia is gradually pushed up and the sac descends.

The surgeon separates the vas deferens and spermatic vessel from the hernia sac. The vas deferens is positioned medially, while the spermatic vessel is laterally. A window is created between the sac and the cord structures, and the dissection continues to separate these anatomical elements.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the inseparable hernia sac in a complete inguinal scrotal hernia, cutting through the dense tissue.
The surgeon enlarges the preperitoneal pocket by carefully separating surrounding tissues, creating sufficient space for the placement of the mesh.

The surgeon identifies the spermatic vessel laterally and the vas deferens medially within the triangle of doom. The dissection continues laterally to enlarge the pocket. The telescope is introduced into the hernia sac to confirm the presence and openness of the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon has completely separated the hernia sac, ensuring there is no visible defect or 'black hole' in the peritoneal side. The sigmoid colon is seen emerging with the peritoneum. The spermatic vessel is positioned laterally and is slightly more separated from the peritoneum.

The surgeon is inside the hernia defect, where the cut part of the sac is visible without serum collection or bleeding. The indirect inguinal hernia sac is observed, along with the spermatic vessel and vas deferens. The triangle of doom and triangle of pain are identified, with the transversus fascia and arc of the transversus abdominal muscle above. The inferior epigastric vessel is clearly visible alongside the vas deferens.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac, with the spermatic vessel and inferior epigastric vessel clearly visible. A mesh is spread over the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster, ensuring it extends six centimeters beyond the defect margins. The center of the mesh is aligned with the defect center. A tacker is used to secure the mesh to the Cooper ligament medially.

The surgeon pushes the inferomedial margin of the sac into the space of Retzius. A tacker is fired laterally on the transversus abdominis muscle and another on the rectus abdominis, just lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, completing the mesh fixation.

The surgeon covers the hernia repair site with the peritoneum, ensuring the mesh is completely covered and not exposed to the abdominal wall. A tacker is used to secure the peritoneum, while gas pressure is reduced to facilitate the process. Care is taken to avoid firing the tacker over the inferior epigastric vessel. The procedure concludes with the removal of gas from the surgical field.
